text: This is a case of a seventy four-year-old male that presented to the emergency room with a GI bleed.

The patient was on anticoagulation and after stopping his anticoagulation, endoscopy was performed and his gastroenterologist did identify a two point five centimeter mass just distal to the pylorus, therefore surgery was consulted.

This is one of the first cases that I really used the TilePro technology robotically and I thought this would be a great opportunity to see if we can combine both the traditional three-dimensional robotic laparoscopic view with a gastroscope to try to really excise this tumor and keeping as much tissue as possible.
Luckily, the gastroenterologist did tattoo this area, and the case was started traditionally as we do any robotic case.
The surgeon replaces the port and uses a vessel sealer to identify the mass within the surgical field.
The surgeon frees the mass located anteriorly on the pylorus, carefully releasing surrounding adhesions to improve access and visibility.
The gastroenterologist performs an endoscopy, inserting the endoscope into the patient's gastrointestinal tract to visualize the internal structures.

The surgeon identifies the mass on the pylorus using the tattoo as a guide, visible through both the traditional laparoscopic view and the gastroscope view on the robotic console. A stay suture is placed in the mass for control.
The surgeon uses a robotic SureForm stapler to excise the mass with a healthy margin of tissue, ensuring minimal removal to avoid the need for formal reconstruction of the gastrointestinal tract.
The surgeon uses a combination of suture traction and counter-traction, with the gastroscope positioned in the lumen of the pylorus, to identify a suitable area for firing the stapler.
The surgeon positions the robotic stapler to excise the mass located anteriorly on the pylorus, ensuring the mass is completely controlled and avoiding closure of the pylorus.
The surgeon ensures an adequate amount of tissue is available for resection, using robotic assistance to maintain precision in the surgical field.

I am a big fan of this Robotic SureForm stapling technology. It does take a lot of guesswork out. I find it has an excellent tissue seal, having a good degree of hemostasis as well, and it helps sense the tissue.
The surgeon ensures that the blue 60 SureForm robotic stapler fires correctly, using two staple loads to excise the mass with viable margins.
The surgeon performs a satisfactory resection using a gastroscope from inside the lumen and a 3D robotic camera from the outside, ensuring precise visualization and control during the procedure.
The freed mass is placed into an endocatch bag. The specimen is then opened on the back table, revealing clear margins.
Subsequently, pathology reviewed the mass and was found to be a neuroendocrine tumor with good margins.
A surgical drain is visibly placed in the patient, followed by the initiation of clear liquid intake on postoperative day one.
The drain was removed postoperative day number two. and the patient overall did very well and was discharged without any incident.

I really believe that this technology helped to really not only give me a view that I wouldn't normally have, seeing both the endoscopic view but the robotic view, and we were able to get just enough tissue to incorporate our mass and also have enough tissue and tissue left behind not to perform any stenosis on this patient, and I was very happy with the results.
So I thought this was an interesting technique, and hopefully will be used in the future for different kinds of cases as well.
